metadata.title: Scarcity, Opportunity Cost, and Incentives
metadata.subtitle: Understanding Why You Can't Have Everything—and How to Make the Best Choices

content.title: Scarcity, Opportunity Cost, and Incentives
content.subtitle: Understanding Why You Can't Have Everything—and How to Make the Best Choices

content.label: The Challenge
content.question:
Sarah, 18, was deciding between Elite Private College ($55,000/year) and State University ($15,000/year).

Simple analysis: Elite costs $160,000 more over four years.

But Sarah calculated the FULL opportunity cost: Elite's total including foregone wages was $372,000 vs State's $252,000—a $120,000 difference.

She chose State and invested the difference. By age 65, that investment grew to $1,800,000.

What are you REALLY giving up when you make financial decisions?

objectives.description: Define scarcity as the fundamental economic problem: unlimited wants exceed limited resources.
Calculate opportunity costs including both explicit and implicit costs for personal finance decisions.
Analyze how incentives shape behavior and predict responses to rewards and penalties.
Apply opportunity cost analysis to major life decisions: college, career, housing, and investments.

terms.definition: The fundamental economic problem: unlimited human wants exceed limited resources, forcing choices and trade-offs.
terms.example: You have $20 and want both lunch ($12) and a movie ($15). Scarcity forces you to choose.

terms.definition: The value of the next-best alternative forgone when making a choice—the TRUE cost of any decision.
terms.example: If you spend Saturday working, the opportunity cost is the concert you couldn't attend.

terms.definition: Direct monetary payments required for a choice—the obvious, out-of-pocket expenses.
terms.example: Tuition, textbooks, car payments, rent—anything you pay cash for.

terms.definition: Indirect costs including time, foregone alternatives, and non-monetary sacrifices.
terms.example: The wages you DON'T earn while in college—often larger than tuition itself.

leftPanel.title: Why Economics Exists
paragraphs: Everyone faces unlimited wants but limited resources:
• Time: Only 24 hours/day. Can't be everywhere at once.
• Money: Finite income, unlimited desires.
• Energy: Limited attention and effort.
Even billionaires face scarcity—they can't buy more time or be in two places at once.
Scarcity forces EVERY decision to involve trade-offs. That's why economics exists.

highlightBox.text: Time is the ultimate scarce resource—everyone gets exactly 24 hours per day, regardless of wealth.

leftColumn.title: Explicit Costs (Obvious)
items: Show up on receipts and bills

rightColumn.title: Implicit Costs (Hidden)
items: Value of what you give up

layoutData.title: Total Economic Cost = Explicit + Implicit
paragraphs: 4-Year University Opportunity Cost Calculation:
bulletPoints: Key insight: The implicit costs (foregone wages) nearly equal the explicit costs (tuition)!

keyPoint.text: 📊 Most people only calculate tuition. Opportunity cost thinking reveals the TRUE cost is $200,000—not $80,000.

content.headerTitle: The Hidden Cost: Your Time Has Value

leftPanel.title: Marcus's Saturday "Savings"
paragraphs: Marcus earns $36/hour but spends 6 hours on Saturday doing home maintenance to "save money."
Cost to hire services: $70/week
His time's opportunity cost: 6 hours × $36 = $216
By doing it himself, Marcus "saved" $70 but gave up $216 in potential earnings.
Net loss: $146/week = $7,600/year

highlightBox.text: The mistake: Looking at absolute cost ($70) instead of opportunity cost ($216).

content.headerTitle: Incentives: Why People Do What They Do

rightColumn.title: Negative Incentives (Discourage)
items: Late fees → Pay bills on time

layoutData.title: The Right Question to Ask
paragraphs: Instead of asking "Can I afford this?" ask: "Is this the best use of my scarce resources?"
bulletPoints: Before: "The car costs $25,000. I can afford the payments."
After: "What else could I do with $25,000? Invest it? Start a business? How much would it be worth in 30 years?"
After: "What am I giving up to take this job? Better opportunities? Time with family? Career growth?"
Before: "I'll save money by doing it myself."
After: "Is my time worth more than the cost to hire someone? What's my opportunity cost?"

keyPoint.text: 💡 Every "yes" to one thing is a "no" to something else. Opportunity cost reveals what you're really giving up.

content.headerTitle: Sunk Costs: What You Should Ignore

leftPanel.title: Money Already Spent Is Gone
paragraphs: Sunk cost fallacy: Continuing to invest in something because you've already put money into it—even when it no longer makes sense.
Example: "I've already spent $2,000 fixing this car, so I should spend another $3,000 to finish."
Reality: The $2,000 is gone either way. The only question is: Is the car worth $3,000 MORE repair, or should you buy something else?
Past spending should NOT influence future decisions. Only compare future costs vs. future benefits.

infoCard.title: Sunk Cost Examples
items: Finishing a bad movie you paid $15 for
Eating more because you paid for a buffet
Staying in a bad major because of credits taken

scenario.name: Sarah's College Decision
paragraphs: Sarah chose State University ($15,000/year) over Elite Private College ($55,000/year).
She invested the $120,000 difference at 7% average return for 47 years (until age 65).

scenario.name: The Chen Family Housing Trade-off
paragraphs: The Chens can afford a $500,000 house with a $2,800/month mortgage.
Alternatively, they could buy a $350,000 house ($2,000/month) and invest the $800/month difference.

content.question:
Your friend says: "I can't leave this job even though I hate it—I've already spent 3 years here!"

Is this a valid reason to stay?

How would you explain the sunk cost fallacy to them? What questions SHOULD they be asking?

Think about a major financial decision you're facing—college, car, job, housing.

What is the explicit cost?
What is the implicit/opportunity cost?

Does calculating the full opportunity cost change your decision?

questions.question: Why does scarcity affect everyone, even billionaires? What resource can nobody buy more of?
If you spend 3 hours "saving" $30 and your time is worth $20/hour, what was the actual cost of "saving" that money?
Why are implicit costs usually larger than explicit costs for major decisions like college?
How do 401(k) matching programs create incentives? What's the opportunity cost of NOT participating?

takeaways.title: Scarcity Forces Choices
takeaways.description: Unlimited wants vs. limited resources means every decision involves trade-offs—even for the wealthy.

takeaways.title: Opportunity Cost Reveals Truth
takeaways.description: The TRUE cost is what you give up. Include implicit costs (time, foregone alternatives) not just explicit costs.

takeaways.title: Ignore Sunk Costs
takeaways.description: Money already spent is gone. Only compare future costs to future benefits when making decisions.

takeaways.title: Incentives Shape Behavior
takeaways.description: People respond to rewards and penalties. Understanding incentives helps predict behavior—including your own.

main.title: Opportunity Cost Calculator
main.description: You've learned to calculate opportunity cost—now apply it to your own life decisions.

steps: Calculate college opportunity cost: tuition + foregone wages + time value = true cost
Analyze your time allocation: List weekly activities and calculate opportunity cost of each
Compare major purchase options: New car vs. used car vs. invest the difference
Evaluate a career change: Investment required vs. lifetime earnings increase
Identify incentives in your life and predict behavioral responses

content.tagline: Building Financial Futures, One Lesson at a Time
